title: A Hymn of Praise: Psalm 8
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-18-24 -PM- A Hymn of Praise - Psalm 8 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Context and Structure of Psalm 8
subpoints: A hymn of praise written for corporate singing — 'our Lord' signals communal worship
Occasion likely rooted in David's shepherd years, meditating on creation under the stars
Structure: an inclusio — verse 1 and verse 9 form the refrain that frames everything; all other verses flow from or into the central claim
Main point: Yahweh's name is excellent (majestic, glorious, splendid) in all the earth

outline.title: 1. God's Name Is Excellent (vv. 1, 9)
subpoints: Two different Hebrew words: 'Yahweh' (God's covenant name) and 'Adonai' (Lord/Master) — 'Yahweh our Master, how excellent is your name'
'Excellent' means magnificent, grand, splendid, imposing, marvelous — terms describing impressive beauty and dignity
God's name is not made excellent but declared excellent by what he reveals of himself — through creation (Romans 1:20) and supremely in Christ
God's glory is set above the heavens — he transcends creation, not merely the biggest creature

outline.title: 2. God's Power Displayed Through the Weak (vv. 2-3)
subpoints: 'Out of the mouth of babes and nursing infants you have ordained strength' — God uses the weakest things to silence his enemies
Paul develops the same theme in 1 Corinthians 1:27-29 — God chose the foolish and weak things to shame the wise and mighty
2 Corinthians 12:9 — Paul's thorn kept him weak so God's strength could be displayed through him
Jesus quotes verse 2 on Palm Sunday (Matthew 21:15-16) — the children's praise silences the chief priests who are God's enemies

outline.title: 3. The Stunning Condescension: God Is Mindful of Man (vv. 4-5)
subpoints: 'What is man that you are mindful of him?' — in the vast grandeur of creation, why should God notice humanity at all?
The honest answer: he shouldn't. The fact that he does is the display of God's gracious condescension
'You have made him a little lower than the angels and crowned him with glory and honor' — it is the image of God (imago Dei) that gives human life its sanctity
The imago Dei is why abortion is wrong, why we love our neighbor, why human life demands dignity

outline.title: 4. Man as Steward of Creation (vv. 6-8)
subpoints: God appointed man to have dominion over all created things — the dominion mandate of Genesis 1:28 was not revoked by the fall
The context in which dominion is given is awe, not pride — in our puniness, God entrusted us with the care of creation, not angels
We are called to be wise stewards: conservative Christians should not throw out a biblical stewardship mandate just because of liberal environmentalism
David writing this as a shepherd sees his own life reflected — a man tending God's flock under God's stars

outline.title: Application: Humility and Awe Before a Majestic God
subpoints: We do not walk around announcing we are the crown rulers of creation — the context is wonder at our own smallness and God's gracious appointment
The excellence of God's name should drive us to worship, not pride
We see God's glory most clearly not in the ocean or the stars, but 'in the face of Jesus Christ' (2 Cor. 4:6)

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on Psalm 8, David's hymn of praise for God's excellent name. The Psalm has an inclusio structure — verses 1 and 9 frame everything with the same refrain, so that all of David's meditation on creation flows from and back into the central declaration: 'O Lord our Lord, how excellent is Your name in all the earth.' The Psalm covers God's transcendent glory set above the heavens, his power displayed through the weakest things (babes and nursing infants silencing his enemies — a verse Jesus quotes on Palm Sunday), the astonishing condescension of a God who is mindful of puny humanity, and the calling of man as steward and crown ruler of creation by virtue of the image of God. The application is one of humility: we are small, creation is vast, and yet God appointed us — a calling received in awe, not pride.
transcript:
## Introduction: A Hymn of Joy

For our Scripture reading this afternoon, we turn to Psalm 8.

> *O Lord our Lord, how excellent is Your name in all the earth, who have set Your glory above the heavens! Out of the mouth of babes and nursing infants You have ordained strength, because of Your enemies, that You may silence the enemy and the avenger. When I consider Your heavens, the work of Your fingers, the moon and the stars, which You have ordained, what is man that You are mindful of him, and the son of man that You visit him? For You have made him a little lower than the angels, and You have crowned him with glory and honor. You have made him to have dominion over the works of Your hands; You have put all things under his feet, all sheep and oxen — even the beasts of the field, the birds of the air, and the fish of the sea that pass through the paths of the seas. O Lord our Lord, how excellent is Your name in all the earth!*

This is a song of David. As the title tells us, it was written to be sung with instruments conveying a joyous melody. Like Psalms 33, 104, and 145, this is a hymn of praise to God for his works of creation.

We may probably conclude that David is drawing from his experience in the field as a shepherd as he writes this. His nights in the open fields with the sheep during the summer months would have given him ample opportunity to explore God's creation — and you can see that motif throughout.

**Structure.** The main point of the Psalm: *God's name is excellent.* You see that in verses 1 and 9. The whole Psalm uses an inclusio — it starts with the refrain and ends with the refrain. Everything in verses 2-8 either flows from or leads back to: Yahweh's name is excellent. How do we know it was written for corporate singing? The pronoun 'our' — David puts the words in the mouths of God's people together.

## 1. God's Name Is Excellent (vv. 1, 9)

Notice that there are two different words translated 'Lord' in verse 1. Our English translations show this by capitalizing all the letters of the first (LORD = Yahweh, God's covenant name) and only the first letter of the second (Lord = Adonai, Master). So David is saying: *Yahweh our Master, your name is excellent.*

The one whose name is excellent is specifically the one who is our Lord, our Master — the owner of those who belong to him. And this God's glory is set *above the heavens*. He transcends creation. You don't get to him by going up through the atmosphere. He is beyond creation — not the biggest creature, but the Creator himself, who has always existed.

The word *excellent* means having or showing impressive beauty and dignity. Synonyms: magnificent, grand, splendid, respondent, glorious, impressive, noble, awe-inspiring, monumental, marvelous. These are the terms that should come to mind when we think of who God is and how he reveals himself.

God's name is declared excellent by what he reveals of himself through creation (Romans 1:20 — the attributes of God are clearly seen in what has been made). Creation still tells that story. Though sin has corrupted our perception, so we need the Spirit to use Scripture to show us more.

## 2. God's Power Displayed Through the Weak (vv. 2-3)

*Out of the mouth of babes and nursing infants You have ordained strength because of Your enemies, that You may silence the enemy and the avenger.*

Can you think of anything weaker than a nursing infant, completely at the mercy of those around it? And yet God uses that to silence his enemies. It is a reference to his sovereign power: even the weakest things in God's hand are more powerful than his adversaries.

Paul develops the same idea in 1 Corinthians 1:27-29: *God has chosen the foolish things of the world to shame the wise; God has chosen the weak things of the world to shame the mighty.* God doesn't use the strong in his service. He uses the weak — so that when he is glorified through them, he gets all the glory and we get all the blessings. Even a donkey can be used by God (Numbers 22). This keeps preachers humble.

Jesus quotes this verse on Palm Sunday (Matthew 21:15-16). The children are crying Hosanna in the temple; the Pharisees demand Jesus silence them. Jesus replies: *If these were silent, the stones would cry out — for out of the mouth of babes You have ordained praise.* He is showing that God silences his enemies through the weakest praises, and that the chief priests, in trying to silence these children, are positioning themselves as God's enemies.

## 3. The Stunning Condescension (vv. 4-5)

*When I consider Your heavens, the work of Your fingers, the moon and the stars which You have ordained — what is man that You are mindful of him, and the son of man that You visit him?*

Note: *the work of Your fingers.* Not the work of God's arm, not his full strength — but a flick of his fingers to create the entire cosmos. The vastness that staggers astronomers, Carl Sagan's 'billions and billions of stars' — a finger-flick.

And then — in the midst of this vastness — God chose *puny men* to be the object of his love and the crown jewel of his creation. The question David asks is a rhetorical exclamation: why should God even look at man, let alone look at him with regard and love? The honest answer is: he shouldn't. It makes no sense. And the fact that he does is the display of God's gracious condescension toward mankind.

*You have made him a little lower than the angels and crowned him with glory and honor.* God created man as the supreme being of the visible realm — just a little lower than the angels. What gives human life its dignity, its sanctity? Nothing else but the *imago Dei* — the image of God. That is why abortion is wrong. That is why we love our neighbor. That is the foundation of human dignity.

## 4. Man as Steward of Creation (vv. 6-8)

*You have made him to have dominion over the works of Your hands; You have put all things under his feet.*

The fall did not revoke God's directive to man. We are still called to be God's stewards of this earth — responsible, wise use and management of his creation. That does not mean preserving everything exactly as it is, but it means being wise about what we leave for the next generation. Conservative Christians are often suspicious of anything called environmentalism, with good reason — but we must not throw out the biblical stewardship mandate with the bathwater of liberal politics.

And notice the context in which dominion is given: awe. Not pride. It humbles us that in our puniness and smallness God entrusted the care of creation to us — not to the angels, who are greater — to us. That is cause for wonder, not arrogance.

## Application: Humility and Awe Before an Excellent Name

With David, we say: *O Lord our Lord — our Master, our creator — how majestic is Your name. Not just here, but in all the earth. You have set Your glory above the heavens, and You have let us see Your glory in the face of Jesus Christ.*

We go out and serve him in this world.

Father, thank you for Psalm 8. Thank you for the inspired words of David. We thank You that through creation we can see that you are the creator of all things. Thank you for opening our eyes to see that. We pray that we would go through life knowing that Your name is excellent, and that we are to steward Your name in this earth. In Jesus' name, amen.
